content: Let's cut through the noise and look at what actually happens under the hood when you call file.metadata() on a Rust file system. The core confusion people have is thinking that metadata is just some magic property stored inside the file itself, but it's not; it's a contract with the operating system to read specific attributes from the inode or directory entry structure on disk. When you invoke std::fs::metadata(), the runtime performs a stat syscall, which returns an io::os::Metadata object that wraps this raw data, and crucially, this operation always follows symlinks by default unless you explicitly use symlink_metadata() to bypass them. This distinction is vital because if your application relies on file size or modification time without checking for symlinks, you might accidentally read metadata from the target of a chain rather than the link itself, leading to subtle bugs when files are replaced but links remain in place. The Permissions struct that emerges from this metadata isn't just a wrapper; it represents the actual Unix mode bits extracted into our safe type system, allowing us to check logical operations like is_readonly() or set permissions using set_permissions(). When you call set_permissions(), Rust constructs an io::os::Permissions object containing those mode bits and sends a chmod syscall back to the kernel, but here's where it gets messy: on Windows, this API behaves very differently because there are no Unix-style file modes in the same way; instead, you're dealing with access token flags and security descriptors that require a much more complex interaction with the system. The mental model you need is not 'Rust reads metadata' but rather 'Rust translates your intent into a platform-specific syscall sequence,' which means you must always account for the possibility that file operations will fail if the underlying OS doesn't support the specific mode bits or permission changes you're requesting. I've seen teams spend weeks debugging why their set_permissions() call fails on production servers only to realize they were blindly applying Linux-style read-write-execute flags onto a Windows filesystem where those flags simply don't exist in the same semantic space. The right approach is to treat file metadata as an external resource that requires careful validation at every step of your application logic, ensuring you never assume uniform behavior across platforms without explicit checks or conditional compilation guards.
